request.question: Are both Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie and Hawaiian Poi Dog extinct breeds of dogs?

title: Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie
Hawaiian Poi Dog
Marquesan Dog
Collie
Poi Dog Pondering
List of dog fighting breeds
Anglo-Français and Français (hound)
Chemin de fer de Petite Ceinture
Tweed Water Spaniel
Teddy Roosevelt Terrier
sentences: The Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie is a medium-sized breed of dog used in hunting as a scenthound, usually in packs.
It is one of the Anglo-French hound breeds which were created by crossing French scenthounds with English (Anglo) foxhounds.
The name "Petite Vénerie" does not mean that dogs of the breed are "petite" or small, but rather that it is used to hunt small game.

The Hawaiian Poi Dog (Hawaiian: "ʻīlio" or "ʻīlio mākuʻe" for brown individuals) is an extinct breed of pariah dog from Hawaiʻi which was used by Native Hawaiians as a spiritual protector of children and as a source of food.
The Marquesan Dog or Marquesas Islands Dog is an extinct breed of dog from the Marquesas Islands.
Similar to other strains of Polynesian dogs, it was introduced to the Marquesas by the ancestors of the Polynesian people during their migrations.
Serving as a tribal totems and religious symbols, they were sometimes consumed as meat although less frequently than in other parts of the Pacific because of their scarcity.
These native dogs are thought to have become extinct before the arrival of Europeans, who did not record their presence on the islands.
Petroglyphic representations of dogs and the archaeological remains of dog bones and burials are the only evidence that the breed ever existed.
Modern dog population on the island are the descendants of foreign breeds later reintroduced in the 19th century as companions for European settlers.
The collie is a distinctive type of herding dog, including many related landraces and standardised breeds.
The type originated in Scotland and Northern England.
The collie is a medium-sized, fairly lightly built dog, with a pointed snout.
Many types have a distinctive white pattern over the shoulders.
Collies are very active and agile, and most types of collies have a very strong herding instinct.
Collie breeds have spread through many parts of the world (especially Australia and North America) and have diversified into many varieties, sometimes with mixture from other dog types.

Some collie breeds have remained as working dogs, used for herding cattle, sheep and other livestock, while others are kept as pets, show dogs or for dog sports, in which they display great agility, stamina and trainability.
While the AKC has a breed they call "Collie", in fact collie dogs are a distinctive type of herding dog including many related landraces and formal breeds.
There are usually major distinctions between show dogs and those bred for herding trials or dog sports.
They typically display great agility, stamina and trainability and more importantly sagacity.
Poi Dog Pondering is an American musical group, noted for its cross-pollination of diverse musical genres, including various forms of acoustic and electronic music.
Founded in Hawaii in 1984 by Frank Orrall, initially as a solo project.
In 1985 Orrall formed the first line-up of PDP to perform its first concert; at the Honolulu Academy of Arts.
The band then embarked on a yearlong Street Performance Busking tour across North America, eventually settling in Austin, Texas in 1987, where they recorded their first three albums.

In 1992, the band relocated, this time to Chicago, Illinois, where they began to incorporate Orchestral arrangements & elements of Electronic, House Music and Soul music into their Acoustic Rock style.
The membership of Poi Dog Pondering has changed from album to album.
This is a list of dog fighting breeds originally developed for, or commonly used at some time in their history for dog fighting.
Extinct breeds are included.
Anglo-Français and Français hounds are a general type of hunting dogs that include ancient French hounds and breeds created by mixing the French dogs with English (Anglo) Foxhounds.
There are seven dog breeds that are described as Anglo-Français and Français hounds.

Paris' former Chemin de fer de Petite Ceinture ('small(er) belt railway'), also colloquially known as "La Petite Ceinture", was a circular railway built as a means to supply the city's fortification walls, and as a connection between Paris' railway termini.

Built as two distinct 'Ceinture Syndicate' freight and 'Paris-Auteuil' passenger lines from 1851 that together formed an arc that surrounded the northern two thirds of Paris, it would become a fill circle of rail around the capital when its third Ceinture Rive Gauche section was built in 1867.
Serving first as a freight-only line, then developing into a passenger service, the Chemin de fer de Ceinture became Paris' first metro-like urban transport.
Much-frequented until its 1900 Universal Exposition peak, the Metro appearing that year would mark the onset of the Petite Ceinture line's decline.
The Tweed Water Spaniel, or Tweed Spaniel, is a breed of dog extinct since the 19th century.
It is best known for being involved in the early development of the modern Curly Coated Retriever and Golden Retriever breeds of dogs.
They were described as a generally brown athletic dogs from the area around Berwick-upon-Tweed near the River Tweed and close to the Scottish Borders.
A type of water dog, the breed was not well known outside the local area.
This breed may have been created by crossing local water dogs with imported St. John's water dog, another breed which is also now extinct.
The Teddy Roosevelt Terrier is a small to medium-sized American hunting terrier.
Lower-set with shorter legs, more muscular, and heavier bone density than its cousin the American Rat Terrier.
There is much diversity in the history of the Teddy Roosevelt Terrier breed and it shares a common early history with the American Rat Terrier, Fox Paulistinha and Tenterfield Terrier.
It is said the Rat Terrier background stems from the terriers or other dogs that were brought over by early English and other working class immigrants.

Since the breed was a farm, hunting and utility dog there was little to no planned breeding other than breeding dogs with agreeable traits to each other in order to produce the desired work ethic in the dog.

It is assumed that the Feist (dog), Bull Terrier, Smooth Fox Terrier, Manchester Terrier, Whippet, Italian Greyhound, the now extinct English White Terrier, Turnspit dog and or Wry Legged Terrier all share in the Teddy Roosevelt Terrier's ancestry.
These early Ratting Terriers were then most likely bred to the Beagle or Beagle cross bred dogs (for increased scenting ability) and other dogs.
Maximizing the influences from these various breeds provides the modern Teddy Roosevelt Terrier with a keen sense of awareness and prey drive, an acute sense of smell and a very high intellect.
Although they tend to be aloof with strangers they are devoted companion dogs with a strong desire to please and be near their owners side at all times.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Are both Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie and Hawaiian Poi Dog extinct breeds of dogs?
hop1_docs: Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie | The Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie is a medium-sized breed of dog used in hunting as a scenthound, usually in packs.  It is one of the Anglo-French hound breeds which were created by crossing French scenthounds with English (Anglo) foxhounds.  The name "Petite Vénerie" does not mean that dogs of the breed are "petite" or small, but rather that it is used to hunt small game.

Hawaiian Poi Dog | The Hawaiian Poi Dog (Hawaiian: "ʻīlio" or "ʻīlio mākuʻe" for brown individuals) is an extinct breed of pariah dog from Hawaiʻi which was used by Native Hawaiians as a spiritual protector of children and as a source of food.

Anglo-Français and Français (hound) | Anglo-Français and Français hounds are a general type of hunting dogs that include ancient French hounds and breeds created by mixing the French dogs with English (Anglo) Foxhounds.  There are seven dog breeds that are described as Anglo-Français and Français hounds.

Petit Bleu de Gascogne | The Petit Bleu de Gascogne (FCI No.31) Is a breed of dog of the scenthound type, originating in France and used for hunting in packs.  Today's breed is the descendant of a very old type of large hunting dog.  The Petit Bleu de Gascogne is not a small (petite) dog, the name comes from its use on small game.

Grand Anglo-Français Blanc et Noir | The Grand Anglo-Français Blanc et Noir (FCI No.323) translated into English as the Great Anglo-French White and Black Hound, is a breed of dog used in hunting as a scenthound, usually in packs.  It is one of the Anglo-French hound breeds which were created by crossing French scenthounds with English (Anglo) Foxhounds.

Grand Anglo-Français Blanc et Orange | The Grand Anglo-Français Blanc et Orange (FCI No.324) translated into English as the Great Anglo-French White and Orange Hound, is a breed of dog used in hunting as a scenthound, usually in packs.  It is one of the Anglo-French hound breeds which were created by crossing French scenthounds with English (Anglo) foxhounds.

Braque Francais | The Braques français are hunting dogs, from a very old type of gun dog used for pointing the location of game birds for a hunter.  There are two breeds of Braque français, both from the south of France, the Braque français, type Gascogne (French Pointing Dog - Gascogne type, larger size) and the Braque français, type Pyrénées (French Pointing Dog - Pyrenean type, smaller size) They are popular hunting dogs in France, but are seldom seen elsewhere.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie — passage [1]: "medium-sized breed of dog used in hunting" (no extinction stated).
  - Hawaiian Poi Dog — passage [2]: "an extinct breed of pariah dog from Hawaiʻi".
- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie: extinction status not given in passages (no explicit "extinct" or "extant").
  - No other pages for Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie present; multiple Anglo-Français breeds exist but passage [1] appears to refer to the named breed.
- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Explicit current status of Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie (extinct vs. extant).
- retrieval_direction (priority order):
  1. Open the "Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie" Wikipedia page (or breed-specific encyclopedia page).
  2. Search query: "Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie extinct" or "status Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie".
  3. Check kennel/FCI breed registry entry: "FCI Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie status" or breed club information.
  4. If needed, search for historical sources on Anglo-Français hounds mentioning disappearance or current populations.
response.hop2_query: Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie extinct status
hop2_docs: Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie | The Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie is a medium-sized breed of dog used in hunting as a scenthound, usually in packs.  It is one of the Anglo-French hound breeds which were created by crossing French scenthounds with English (Anglo) foxhounds.  The name "Petite Vénerie" does not mean that dogs of the breed are "petite" or small, but rather that it is used to hunt small game.

Anglo-Français and Français (hound) | Anglo-Français and Français hounds are a general type of hunting dogs that include ancient French hounds and breeds created by mixing the French dogs with English (Anglo) Foxhounds.  There are seven dog breeds that are described as Anglo-Français and Français hounds.

Lycée Français de Palma | Lycée Français de Palma (LFP; Spanish: Liceo francés de Palma ) is a French international school in El Terreno (/), Palma de Mallorca, Spain.  It serves "toute petite section" through "lycée" (senior high school/sixth form college).

Lycée Français de Bilbao | Lycée Français de Bilbao is a French international school in Zamudio, Biscay, Spain, in the Bilbao metropolitan area.  It serves "toute petite section" through "terminale" (final year of "lycée" or senior high school/sixth form).

Lycée Français de Málaga | Lycée Français de Málaga (Spanish: Liceo Francés de Málaga ) is a French international school in Málaga, Spain.  It serves levels "petite section" through "terminale", the final year of "lycée" (senior high school/sixth form college).  It occupies a former sports club.

Lycée Français de Valence | Lycée Français de Valence (LFV; Spanish: Liceo Francés de Valencia ) is a French international school in Paterna, Spain, near Valencia.  It serves "petite section" of "maternelle"/"infantil" (preschool) through "terminale", the final year of "lycée"/"bachillerato" (senior high school/sixth form college).

Lycée Français de Gavà Bon Soleil | Lycée Français de Gavà Bon Soleil (LFG) is a French international school in Gavà, Barcelona Province, Catalonia, Spain.  it serves "petite section" through "seconde", the second-to-last year of "lycée" (sixth form college/high school).  The school serves ages 3–18.
hop2_titles: Lycée Français de Gavà Bon Soleil
response.summary_2: No. The Hawaiian Poi Dog is described as an extinct breed of pariah dog from Hawaiʻi. The Anglo-Français de Petite Vénerie is described as a medium-sized hunting scenthound, and none of the provided passages state it is extinct.
